planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; Brazilian stylo is the most widely grown tropical pasture and cover crop legume in the world, used across Australia, Africa, and Southeast Asia to improve nitrogen-depleted tropical soils and as a protein-rich, drought-tolerant component of tropical pasture systems.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed at 2–5 lbs per acre in warm, well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; inoculate with appropriate Rhizobium before sowing. Stylosanthes guianensis (Brazilian stylo or common stylosanthes) is a semi-erect to sprawling South American tropical legume that has become one of the most globally important tropical cover crops, pasture species, and soil improvement plants. It is grown extensively throughout northern Australia (where it was introduced as a pasture legume), sub-Saharan Africa, and Southeast Asia to improve nitrogen-depleted, acidic, infertile tropical soils. The plant fixes significant quantities of nitrogen through root nodule symbiosis with specific Rhizobium strains, makes excellent protein-rich grazing for livestock, and builds organic matter over time. Stylosanthes is notably tolerant of low-fertility, acidic soils and drought — it thrives on marginal lands where few other legumes survive. Dozens of improved cultivars have been selected for disease resistance (especially anthracnose), yield, and adaptation to different soil types and rainfall patterns. In garden use in zone 9–12, it functions as an outstanding summer green manure on poor, sandy, acidic soils — sow in spring, incorporate at flowering in 60–90 days before planting a food crop.
sources: Australian Centre for International Agricultural Research (ACIAR) — Stylosanthes

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; calopo is a fast-growing, vining South American legume cover crop widely used as an understory nitrogen-fixing groundcover in rubber, oil palm, and cacao plantations across Southeast Asia — it covers the soil rapidly, suppresses weeds, and fixes substantial nitrogen.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed at 3–5 lbs per acre in warm, well-drained, moderately fertile soil; calopo establishes quickly and smothers weeds as a living mulch. Calopogonium mucunoides (calopo or calopogonium) is a trailing and twining annual vine legume from South America that has become one of the most important tropical plantation cover crops in Southeast Asia, particularly Indonesia and Malaysia. It is grown as an understory cover crop beneath rubber trees (Hevea brasiliensis) and oil palms (Elaeis guineensis), where it rapidly covers bare soil between tree rows, fixing nitrogen from the atmosphere through root nodule bacteria, suppressing weeds, preventing soil erosion during tropical monsoon rains, and building soil organic matter as it dies and decomposes. The vine produces attractive, soft-hairy, trifoliate leaves and small, blue-purple flowers; it sprawls to 3–6 feet, rooting at nodes to form a continuous groundcover. It is suited for areas that receive moderate to high rainfall and prefer partial shade as found under plantation canopy. Calopo can be incorporated as a green manure at flowering for maximum nitrogen benefit. A staple of tropical sustainable agriculture that is little-known in North American horticulture.
sources: FAO — Legume Cover Crops in Tropical Agriculture

common_name: Centro (Trailing Centro, Common Butterfly Pea Vine)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; centro is a trailing, twining tropical South American legume cover crop widely used in tropical pasture improvement and as a soil-covering, nitrogen-fixing companion plant in tropical plantation agriculture — it is distinct from Clitoria ternatea (blue butterfly pea) despite a similar common name.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed at 3–5 lbs per acre in warm, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; centro establishes a persistent perennial groundcover in the tropics. Centrosema pubescens (centro or trailing centro) is a sprawling, twining legume vine from tropical South America that has been introduced throughout the tropics as a versatile cover crop and pasture improvement plant. It is widely established in Australia, Southeast Asia, and Africa as a component of improved tropical pastures, where it provides high-protein forage and fixes nitrogen alongside tropical grasses. The plant produces trifoliate leaves and attractive lavender-to-white butterfly-shaped flowers (similar in appearance to those of Clitoria ternatea but botanically distinct — Centrosema is a different genus). As a cover crop in tropical zone 10–12 gardens and plantations, centro is perennial and persistent, forming a trailing groundcover that suppresses weeds and continuously fixes nitrogen through the warm season. It tolerates moderate shade under tree canopies and can be grown in mixed plantings. Seed should be inoculated with appropriate Bradyrhizobium strains before sowing for best nitrogen fixation.
sources: Queensland Department of Agriculture — Tropical Pasture Legumes
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Tropical Cover Crops

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moist soil in full sun; tropical kudzu (Pueraria phaseoloides) is a Southeast Asian vine legume — the agricultural workhorse cover crop of tropical rubber and oil palm plantations worldwide, fixing large amounts of nitrogen and forming a dense, weed-suppressing groundcover; it is biologically distinct from the invasive kudzu (P. montana) of the southeastern United States.

directions: Direct sow in warm, moderately fertile, moist soil in full sun; inoculate seed with Bradyrhizobium before planting; plant in contained areas as the vine can spread aggressively. Pueraria phaseoloides (tropical kudzu or puero) is a Southeast Asian legume vine and the dominant cover crop species in tropical rubber (Hevea) and oil palm (Elaeis) plantation agriculture throughout Malaysia, Indonesia, and across equatorial Asia and Africa. It was deliberately introduced throughout the tropics as the premier nitrogen-fixing, weed-suppressing, soil-protecting cover crop for plantation agriculture — where it forms a continuous 12–24 inch deep groundcover between tree rows that prevents soil erosion during heavy monsoon rains, fixes 100–300 lbs of nitrogen per acre per year, and builds organic matter as older plant material decomposes. Tropical kudzu is biologically distinct from the invasive kudzu of the eastern United States (P. montana var. lobata) — it lacks the tremendous invasive vigor of its relative, though it does spread readily in favorable tropical conditions and should be managed responsibly. The plant produces attractive large, trifoliate leaves and purple flowers similar to common kudzu. Of all tropical nitrogen-fixing cover crops, puero is arguably the most widely used and most agronomically important globally.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Tropical Legumes

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; perennial horsegram is an East African legume used as a drought-tolerant, perennial tropical pasture cover crop in Queensland, Australia and across Africa and Southeast Asia — it excels in low-rainfall, infertile, acidic conditions where other tropical legumes cannot establish.

directions: Direct sow in lean, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; scarify seed or soak 24 hours before planting. Macrotyloma axillare (perennial horsegram or axillaris) is an East African legume that has been widely adopted as a drought-tolerant, perennial tropical pasture cover crop in Queensland, Australia, where it was introduced and extensively trialed as a low-maintenance, nitrogen-fixing component of improved subtropical pastures. The cultivar 'Archer' was selected in Australia for superior persistence, drought tolerance, and productivity in dry subtropical conditions. Unlike annual tropical cover crops, perennial horsegram persists in the soil from one season to the next, regrowth from the root crown after dry-season dormancy — providing a permanent nitrogen-fixing cover without annual replanting costs. It is notably tolerant of acid, infertile, low-phosphorus soils where other legumes fail to establish. The plant produces twining stems with small trifoliate leaves and inconspicuous cream-colored flowers. In zone 9–12 gardens, it is an excellent choice for permanent cover under fruit trees on lean, acidic soil, where it builds fertility year by year without intervention.
sources: Queensland Department of Agriculture — Tropical Pasture Legumes
Australian Tropical Crops and Forages Database
